data.title: Macquarie Strategic Income Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

Economic growth in the US slowed further in 1Q25, continuing a trend that began in the second half of 2023. The slowdown became more apparent during the quarter, possibly reflecting consumer and business caution due to rising uncertainty around the Trump administration’s policy agenda (both in terms of trade policies and government spending cuts). The US Federal Reserve (Fed) maintained its restrictive monetary policies, which have continued to weigh on growth, although fiscal policy has remained supportive. Consumer spending slowed somewhat, and while this followed robust spending in 4Q24, a cooling labor market could cause consumers to remain cautious in the months ahead. Beyond this, while tariffs are likely to weigh on US growth, a resumption in the Fed’s current easing cycle, along with renewed fiscal stimulus, should provide a material positive offset for economic activity in the medium term.

On inflation, the Core Consumer Price Index (Core CPI) again saw a significant increase in January- repeating the start-of-year pattern of recent years- although this was followed by a more subdued reading in February.The Fed’s preferred Core Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) inflation measure indicated that inflation has been better contained over recent months, although the February reading (in contrast to the CPl) was elevated.The more modest pace of demand, ongoing supply expansion, and a labor market that continues to soften should maintain the broader downward trend in inflation. Even so, the path towards the Fed target looks to be slower and bumpier than was earlier anticipated. Labor demand has continued to cool as hiring trends slow, and labor supply has continued to grow, even as immigration has declined. With the resulting better balance in the labor market, wage growth to continue to ease. While tariffs should boost prices in the coming months, we believe that is likely to have only a one-off impact, especially with most measures of inflation expectations remaining well anchored. Even this one-off impact will likely be tempered by the negative impact tariffs may have on both global and US activity.

Outside the US, euro-zone data continued to point toward subdued economic activity growth in that region, while Chinese growth seemed to benefit somewhat from the stimulus measures announced in 2024. In Europe, the German economic outlook remains concerning in the near term, with structural headwinds buffeting key industries including automobiles and chemicals. Further downside risks stem from the US administration’s trade policies.While these all point to downside risks in coming months, we believe a substantially more supportive fiscal outlook will boost growth beyond this with significant government spending on defense and infrastructure now planned over the next few years. Given the degree of slack currently present in the German economy, we do not anticipate materially higher inflation.The European Central Bank (ECB), after cutting rates by 100 basis points in 2024, followed this up with additional 25 basis-point rate cuts in January and March (one basis point is a hundredth of a percentage point). An additional two-and-a-half 25 basis-point cuts over the remainder of 2025 are now priced into rates markets.

The sharp rise in German bond yields that we saw following the recent fiscal-spending announcement serves as a reminder that fiscal outlooks remain a potential cause for concern in many European countries. Europe also remains susceptible to geopolitical events that can affect energy security, with the Russia-Ukraine war continuing to be a potential source of inflationary concern, along with ongoing tension in the Middle East. While the stimulus measures announced by a range of Chinese policymakers over recent months are helping to stabilize activity in the near term, the structural headwinds facing the Chinese economy remain strong, especially the ongoing and deep-seated problems in the property sector. In contrast to most other major central banks, economic conditions in Japan, especially wage and inflation outcomes, are pushing the Bank of Japan (BoJ) to further increase rates as it continues to move away from its previous negative-rate policies. After raising rates in January, the rates markets expect a further increase in the months ahead.

While the higher bond yields at the start of 2025 provided attractive entry levels, the subsequent decline in yields since then suggests a more cautious stance is warranted. We will continue to consider increasing duration as yields rise but also reduce exposure when the market looks rich. In addition, geopolitical considerations such as the new US administration’s policies are increasingly affecting bond yields, and the response of other countries to these policies will also be important. On credit, while the risk aversion we saw in markets has caused spreads to widen somewhat, this comes from a historically tight starting point. While risks to growth have increased, the prospect of further policy support (both monetary and fiscal) is likely to benefit the medium-term growth outlook. Moreover, while spreads remain relatively tight despite the recent widening, all-in yields remain high relative to recent history and expectations of positive total returns should drive demand.

Within the Fund

For 1Q25, Macquarie Strategic Income Fund (MUTF:DUGIX) Institutional Class shares underperformed the Fund’s benchmark, the Bloomberg US Aggregate Index.

Key contributors to performance included:

● Yield curve positioning relative to the benchmark

● An underweight allocation to investment grade corporates

Key detractors from performance included:

● A structurally short duration position compared with the benchmark

•An overweight allocation to high yield corporates

● An underweight allocation to Treasurys

Outlook

In 1Q25, investors shifted their focus to the potentially deleterious effects of Trump’s policies, including tariff wars, In 1Q25, investors shifted their focus to the potentially deleterious effects of Trump’s policies, including tariff wars, federal layoffs, and spending cuts. As a result, markets pulled back from historically rich valuations. Consumer and business sentiment was noticeably weaker, even as hard data remained resilient. Clarity around the scope of potential changes would help assuage market concerns but that may take some time to emerge. Meanwhile, there appears to be some agreement between market participants and the Fed that there will likely be two further cuts this year. That said, the potential for unexpected developments in the economy and the Fed’s reaction remains a concern for us.

Our strategy remains a defensive one, targeting attractive spread and yield advantage over the benchmark while maintaining high levels of liquidity. We tactically added some risk in the recent bout of market volatility but remain at the low end of risk historically. We have moved duration from a modest long to closer to home.

The yield curve is a line that plots the interest rates, at a set point in time, of bonds having equal credit quality, but differing maturity dates.The most frequently reported yield curve compares the 3-month, 2-year, 5-year, and 30-year U.S.Treasury debt.This yield curve is used as a benchmark for other debt in the market, such as mortgage rates or bank lending rates. It is also used to predict changes in economic output and growth. The shape of the yield curve is closely scrutinized because it helps to give an idea of future interest rate change and economic activity. There are three main types of yield curve shapes:normal, inverted and flat (or humped).A normal yield curve is one in which longer maturity bonds have a higher yield compared to shorter term bonds due to the risks associated with time. An inverted yield curve is one in which the shorter-term yields are higher than the longer-term yields, which can be a sign of upcoming recession. A flat (or humped) yield curve is one in which the shorter- and longer-term yields are very close to each other, which is also a predictor of an economic transition.The slope of the yield curve is also seen as important: the greater the slope, the greater the gap between short- and long-term rates.Duration measures a bond’s sensitivity to interest rates, by indicating the approximate percentage of change in a bond or bond fund’s price given a 1% change in interest rates.

data.title: Macquarie Smid Cap Core Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

The Fund’s benchmark, the Russell 2500TM Index, declined 7.50% during the first quarter of 2025. Smid-cap value stocks outpaced smid-cap growth stocks as the Russell 2500TM Value Index declined-7.74% and the Russell 2500TM Growth Index declined-10.80%. The large-cap Russell 1000® Index declined-4.49% while the small-cap Russell 2000® Index (RTY) declined-9.48% for the quarter.

US equity markets began the year on a positive note but turned negative in February amid political uncertainty and weaker-than-expected macroeconomic data. Trade wars dominated headlines after President Trump announced sweeping tariffs on goods imported from China, Mexico, Canada, and the European Union. The market sought clarity as the types of goods, target countries, tariff levels, and deadlines changed multiple times throughout the quarter.Immigration policies, federal government cost-cutting initiatives by the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE), and the ongoing Ukraine war added to the political uncertainty. Hotter-than-expected inflation, weakening consumer sentiment, and softer housing data led to worries of stagflation or even a potential recession.

Amid the market sell-off, defensive sectors, including utilities, consumer staples, and REITs, were among the best-performing within the Russell 2500 Index. Utilities were positive during the quarter. Transportation was the worst-performing sector, while technology and consumer discretionary were also weak.

Within the Fund

For 1Q25, Macquarie Smid Cap Core Fund (MUTF:IVVIX) declined, although it outperformed its benchmark, the Russell 2500 Index, which also declined.

Stock selection contributed to relative performance and was strongest within healthcare, consumer services, and consumer discretionary. Business services and basic materials were the biggest detractors from relative performance.

Within the healthcare sector, notable performers included Intra-Cellular Therapies, Inc., Halozyme Therapeutics, Inc. (HALO), and Insmed Inc. (INSM) Intra-Cellular Therapies was acquired by Johnson & Johnson for $14.1 billion in cash. Halozyme licenses its Enhanze auto-injector technology, which enables the conversion of intravenous (IV) formulated biologic and small molecule drugs to be delivered via subcutaneous injection.The technology improves patient convenience and tolerability, and for drug developers extends market exclusivity beyond expiration of patent protection on the partner’s original proprietary IV drug. During the quarter, Halozyme reported 4Q 2024 financial results above consensus expectations, with royalties and adjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization (EBITDA) exceeding the prior guidance ranges. Most importantly, the company reiterated its guidance for 2025 and its longer-term outlook (2026-28).

Insmed focuses on developing innovative therapeutics for rare pulmonary diseases, and its promising pipeline contributed to the Fund’s performance. During the quarter, the FDA granted priority review for the company’s New Drug Application for brensocatib for patients with non-cystic fibrosis bronchiectasis.

Beacon Roofing Supply, Inc. was the top overall contributor for the period after the company agreed to be acquired by QXO, Inc. (QXO) Brinker International, Inc. (EAT), the parent company of Chili’s and Maggiano’s restaurants, was the strongest performer in the consumer services sector. The company exceeded lofty market expectations with exemplary 31.4% same-store-sales growth at Chili’s, driven by a 19.9% increase in traffic. Chili’s has impressively executed on its marketing initiatives, operating improvements, and menu enhancements. In consumer discretionary, BJ’s Wholesale Club Holdings, Inc. (BJ) was the leading contributor in consumer discretionary sector, as the company reported positive traffic momentum, membership fee income growth, and store expansion plans.

On the negative side, Semtech Corporation (SMTC) was the largest detractor from performance in 1Q. In February, the company revealed that it would not meet its low-end revenue estimates for its active copper cable (ACC) product line due to changes to datacenter rack architecture by a major customer. Although this segment accounts for less than 10% of Semtech’s revenue, the stock corrected by more than 40% due to the product ties to the Al buildout. Despite this setback, we believe the original thesis remains intact, supported by recovery in Semtech’s other end market segments.

Footwear manufacturer, Steve Madden, Ltd. (SHOO), was negatively impacted by the threat of potential tariffs. To mitigate the tariff impact, the company has been aggressively diversifying production out of China. It has already reduced the percentage of goods imported from China from 71% to 58% and is targeting low-40s levels by November 2025. Although the company had planned to shift production to Mexico, it is now monitoring the tariff situation and engaging with factories to seek price concessions. It plans to selectively raise prices on certain products to protect margins.

In capital goods, Tetra Tech, Inc. (TTEK), Federal Signal Corporation (FSS), and Regal Rexnord Corporation (RRX) were the largest detractors. Government spending cuts weighed on both Tetra Tech and Federal Signal, while Regal Rexnord reported earnings below market expectations.

Outlook

We continue to believe that small-cap stocks remain relatively attractive given current valuations, earnings estimates, and the prospect for further rate cuts by the Fed. The forward price/earnings ratio for the Russell 2500® Index stands at a multiple of 14.9, as compared with the Russell 1000® Index at a multiple of 20.3. Additionally, earnings growth is expected to improve over the coming year, with small caps earnings growing faster than large caps, providing small caps a much-needed fundamental catalyst. At the time of writing, FactSet estimates 2025 earnings per share growth for Russell 2000® of 18.5% versus Russell 1000® estimates of 11.3%. Given the current economic backdrop, we fully anticipate downward revisions to earnings estimates and will continue to monitor the economic and political developments.

Markets year-to-date have been focused on the economic impacts of the new political administration. We believe that President Trump’s policy agenda is generally supportive for US small-cap equities, but not without risks. Lower taxes and deregulation should improve profit margins, increase mergers and acquisitions activity, and propel economic growth.However, we believe tariffs and immigration policies are likely negative for small-cap equities. Shortly after quarter end, President Trump announced far-reaching retaliatory tariffs that exceeded market expectations.The initial market response has been sharply negative as fears of economic slowdown and potential recession have increased substantially.We do not expect an immediate or deep recession, yet we recognize the emerging risks and anticipate persistent market volatility. While we continue to unpack the implications, our focus remains on identifying mispriced companies with what we view as attractive growth prospects, sustainable business models, and identifiable catalysts. We find that market volatility and sentiment shifts often present great mispricing opportunities for disciplined, long-term investors.

In April 2025, Macquarie Group Limited (OTCPK:MCQEF) and Nomura Holding America Inc. (NMR) (Nomura) announced that they had entered into an agreement for Nomura to acquire Macquarie Asset Management’s US and European public investments business. The transaction is subject to customary closing conditions, including the receipt of applicable regulatory, client, and shareholder approvals. Subject to such approvals and the satisfaction of these conditions, the transaction is expected to close by the end of 2025.

data.title: Randstad: Fighting In A Fierce Environment Is Too Costly For Investors
data.description:
Introduction

The global leader among HR services and staffing companies is the Dutch Randstad N.V. (RANJF, OTCPK:RANJY), with yearly revenue that peaked at EUR 25.4B in 2023 and then moderated to EUR 24.1B at the end of last year, as labor demand across many economies moderated and softened. The company operates in temporary and permanent placement, specialized staffing, recruiting process outsourcing, and HR solutions, including payroll administration, employee upskilling programs, and engagement tools. As we can understand, this kind of business is highly replicable. As such, the industry is highly fragmented, with Randstad holding around 4% of the global market share. As a matter of fact, many local and regional players are deeply rooted and have earned the trust of local labor market players. Therefore, one of the main strategies a big group like Randstad follows to grow is to continuously acquire new businesses, striving to consolidate the industry, thus eliminating local players. Competition in the industry is fierce, up to the point some consider it cut-throat. Speed of execution, pricing, and low barriers to entry contribute all to a fragmented market where no one truly commands.

Last year, and so far, the first half of 2025, have caused some pains for staffing agencies as the so-called "age of uncertainty" has unfolded. On one side, demand for highly skilled workers stays strong and partly unmet. On the other side, geopolitical tensions (wars, tariffs, raw materials supply chain control, etc.) are impacting directly and indirectly several industries and the end-consumer.

Randstad's Latest Financials

If we read Randstad's Q1 2025 earnings call transcript, we will immediately notice that the word "stabilization" is frequently used to emphasize how the company has stopped bleeding and to reassure investors that the worst seems behind. However, when we look at the report, stabilization means that, across most markets, Randstad's sales are decreasing at a slower pace compared to the previous quarters. Still, we are talking about a business that is slowing down.

When we look at the top line, Randstad reported a 4.2% YoY decrease to EUR 5.66B, with gross profit margin compression of 70 bps to 19.3%. Gross margin was hit by a poorer business mix and idle time, which for a company such as Randstad, represents a true danger.

Randstad's free cash flow was EUR 59M in the quarter because the operating working capital decreased from EUR 144M to EUR 89M.

The performance by geography shows well how Randstad is mainly exposed to Europe, with Southern Europe currently being the largest revenue stream and the largest contributor to the company's EBITA.

When we look at what is going on in the main regions, we see that Northern Europe, a key area for Randstad, is seeing the Netherlands down 7% YoY, Germany down 10% YoY, Belgium down 3% YoY, and the other countries in the area down 4% YoY. Germany, in particular, shows no real improvement as its economy is trying to figure out a way out of the automotive crisis. At the same time, the domestic market for Randstad shows more resilience and has been able to show increasing profitability.

Southern Europe is doing better, especially thanks to Spain and Italy, where Randstad's business has been exceptionally strong since the pandemic. Probably, the huge investments linked to the Recovery and Resilience Plan approved by the EU after the pandemic are still a big tailwind for these two economies. In these two countries, Randstad still achieves a 5.8% EBITA margin, which is quite good for this industry.

North America remains a pain in the neck, with organic revenue growth down 4% YoY in the U.S. and down 6% YoY in Canada. The EBITA margin improved to 3.2%, however, representing a 90 bps increase YoY.

So, right now, we see a decelerating or, at least, stagnant business with little fault of execution. Frankly speaking, there isn't much Randstad can do to change and improve the economic environment.

It is no surprise, then, that this year's regular dividend was only EUR 1.62 while last year's was EUR 2.28, with the addition of a special dividend paid in October of EUR 1.27 per share. This year, I frankly don't know if we will even see a special dividend in October, as we have seen since 2017, with the only pause during the pandemic.

When we read Randstad's 2025 outlook, we see that Q2 was expected to be lower QoQ, and we have little color on what the rest of the year might look like. For sure, automotive is causing quite a bit of concern as the whole supply chain in Europe seems to be more or less stuck in the mud, so to speak.

There might be two trends that will come to Randstad's aid. AI-skill scarcity could increasingly be felt among different industries and businesses as AI tools expand, proliferating and permeating more and more roles and jobs. Here, Randstad could offer upskilling and reskilling services, which could meet the needs of many employers. The second one is linked to a growing work-life balance emphasis, which is becoming more and more important for the younger generations, especially Gen Z.

Randstad's Valuation

Is Randstad currently a buy? The stock, after peaking in mid-2021, has been an overall poor investment, although it has offered some good short-term plays when it rebounded between late 2022 and early 2023 and in October 2023. Right after tariffs were announced, the stock plummeted and has since then almost recovered to its previous levels. However, the valuation is hard to decipher. Due to very poor earnings, the TTM PE seems absurd and stands above 70. The fwd PE is way more reasonable: 22.4. However, this is a very high multiple for a company that operates in a highly competitive market and has low margins. Moreover, it is a multiple that could be justified when factoring in growth, which, at the moment, is hard to predict. Randstad's P/FCF is also a demanding 15.5. Although Randstad is subject to cyclical swings, they are rarely as abrupt as one would imagine looking at these multiples. In other words, when the cycle becomes favorable, Randstad is not a company that usually posts 25% YoY growth for a year or two, thus compressing quickly its past multiples.

Moreover, Randstad's dividend, which is one of the main factors that attract investors to this kind of company, is linked to the company's earnings. During softer periods, the dividend will also be lower. This means that investors will get a low, if not negative, capital return with the addition of dividend cuts. Although Randstad is a very good company, it is not the investment I want to make in today's environment. Its prospects are too uncertain, and the chances of continued underperformance are high. I would profit from the recent rebound and sell out of the stock to deploy the proceeds elsewhere. This is why I am downgrading it to a sell.

data.title: Abercrombie & Fitch: Hollister Is Laying The Groundwork For A Rebound
data.description:
With the stock market continuing to chase new highs, I am prioritizing a rotation of my portfolio into value stocks. Often this means diving into fallen angels that have receded from the heyday of their popularity: but in my view, that's better than holding growth stocks with rich premium that are subject to downward multiples contractions.

Abercrombie & Fitch (NYSE:ANF), the clothing retailer that has worked hard over the past few years to rehabilitate its image, is a great value play. The company is bucking the trend of its industry by posting healthy positive comp sales growth, while also having a plan in place to limit exposure to tariffs. Since the start of the year, shares of Abercrombie have lost ~50% of their value: creating a well-timed buying opportunity for investors who don't mind near-term volatility.

I last wrote a buy opinion on Abercrombie in April, when the stock was still trading in the low $70s. Since then, I've made modest gains on my position, and yet with clear evidence that Abercrombie continues to drive strong growth by refreshing its collection and emphasizing its younger, more casual Hollister brand, I think there's still plenty of room for Abercrombie to rebound higher. Especially with value stocks so rare to find in today's market, I am reiterating my buy position on this stock.

To me, these are the core bull case drivers for Abercrombie & Fitch:

  • Rapid growth via Hollister. Hollister, once the company's junior brand, is now becoming the dominant growth story for Abercrombie & Fitch (and arguably one with less historical baggage than its parent brand). Hollister is achieving very healthy >20% y/y comparable sales growth, more than offsetting milder declines at Abercrombie & Fitch.
  • Value offerings resonating at Hollister? Abercrombie & Fitch, like the rest of the consumer retail industry, is facing a weaker macroeconomy with weaker consumer spending. And yet, despite this, it's clear that Hollister sales are taking off: perhaps because of the brand's positioning as fresh and cool, but at affordable prices.
  • Huge buyback plan. As an indication of management's confidence in the stock's undervaluation, Abercrombie and Fitch has an open, multi-year $1.3 billion share buyback program (roughly worth one-third of the company's current market cap). It plans to execute around ~$400 million in buybacks this year, which is amply supported by the ~$600 million of cash that the company has on its balance sheet (which is now unencumbered of debt).
  • Clear tariff impacts. Unlike many retail companies that have hemmed and hawed on the impacts of tariffs, Abercrombie has made clear what it expects the downside from tariffs to be (assuming current policies remain in place). Assuming 30% tariffs on China and 10% on all other importing markets, the company is expecting a $50 million hit from tariffs, representing 100bps of gross margin - which is a hit, to be sure, but not one that can't be readily absorbed.

The biggest advantage of all to investing in Abercrombie & Fitch is its raw value. At current share prices just north of $80, Abercrombie trades at a market cap of $3.94 billion. After we net off the $608 million of cash on Abercrombie's balance sheet (which is free of debt), the company's resulting enterprise value is $3.33 billion.

Meanwhile, for FY25, Abercrombie has actually boosted its full-year revenue growth expectations from a prior view of 3-5% y/y to 3-6%, or a potential one-point upside at the high end of the range. That being said, it is now also factoring in 100bps of tariff impacts, bringing down its operating margin guidance to a midpoint of 13.0%, and a slightly higher tax rate, leading to a reduction in its pro forma EPS range to $9.50-$10.50: the midpoint of which is an -8% cut from its prior viewpoint.

In spite of this guidance cut, the fact that Abercrombie trades at just an 8.3x FY25 P/E ratio (or even better at 7.0x on an ex-cash basis, when we include the benefit of Abercrombie's ironclad balance sheet) is too good to ignore. Yes, it's true that the company faces macro impacts from slower consumer spending - but that being said, so does every other consumer retail company.

Abercrombie is still growing via its Hollister subsidiary, has a strong handle on how to manage tariffs this year, and is generating substantial profitability with a very clean balance sheet: all in all, I see plenty of reasons to stay invested here and to keep holding out for a broader rebound.

Q1 recap

Let's now go through Abercrombie's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Abercrombie's revenue grew 7% y/y to $1.097 billion, beating Wall Street's expectations of $1.061 billion (+4% y/y) by a respectable three-point margin. Equally, worth noting is the fact that Abercrombie's pace of growth didn't materially decelerate from a 9% growth pace in Q4, despite the increasing macroeconomic headwinds.

The chart below, meanwhile, showcases the company's growth trends by core brand.

We do have to call out one risk here: the core Abercrombie brand slowed to a -10% comparable sales trend in Q1. As a reminder, comparable sales measures the revenue growth from existing store locations, excluding the contribution from newly opened and newly closed stores. It's clear here that the company's flagship brand is losing momentum.

That being said, Hollister is doing an incredible job at picking up the pace and helping to offset Abercrombie's declines. Hollister brand comp sales grew 23% y/y, which has been accelerating from a mid-teens pace in earlier 2024. Over the past two quarters, Hollister has actually delivered more revenue for the overall company than Abercrombie has.

Per CEO Fran Horowitz's remarks on the Q1 earnings call, detailing the core drivers behind growth for Hollister and declines for Abercrombie:

Hollister led the way, delivering record first quarter results with 22% net sales growth last year on top of 12% growth in the first quarter of 2024. We had strong comparable sales as well, up 23%. I'm so proud of the Hollister team as they delivered the brand's eighth consecutive quarter of growth. Both AUR and units were up in the quarter and growth was balanced across genders and categories, with strength in fleece, jeans and skirts. Cross-channel traffic was strong in the quarter and we continue to ramp marketing investment year-over-year to support growth. We're excited about the balance we are seeing in the assortment and we look forward to the summer season officially kicking off.

At Abercrombie Brands, results fell short of expectations. We saw a 4% net sales decline against stellar 31% growth and record net sales achieved in Q1 2024. Comparable sales were down 10% versus 29% comp growth last year as we expected entering the quarter. Sales performance was primarily driven by AUR decline as we moved through winter carryover inventory. We also saw softer results in some of the spring categories that produced standout growth in Q1 last year."

We expect Abercrombie to continue modest declines but stabilize, while Hollister still has plenty of white space for expansion, especially overseas.

We should point out one additional risk here: Abercrombie's cost of sales rose 440bps y/y to 38.0%, indicating a slight deterioration in gross margins (which will be stacked on top of tariff impacts as we move into the back half of the year). The company notes that it's rapidly shifting its product assortment to styles that are resonating with its consumers - namely, Boho and Western fashion styles. It's clearing through old stock now, with the hope that it will have the right assortment for the 2H that will have a better margin profile.

Operating margins also fell -340bps y/y to 9.4%, while on nominal terms, operating income dollars fell -22% y/y to $101.5 million. Still, we note that Abercrombie's pro forma EPS of $1.59 handily beat Wall Street expectations of $1.36 by a rich 17% margin.

Risks and key takeaways

As mentioned, investors should keep an eye out for declines in the Abercrombie brand, plus the recent downward trend on margins. That being said, when we consider those risks against Hollister's ~20% growth and the stock's overall ~8x forward P/E (~7x ex-cash) valuations, we think risks are quite appropriately priced into the stock with plenty of room for upside.

To me, Abercrombie & Fitch is an excellent medium to long-term hold that offers great value in an otherwise very expensive stock market. Buy here with confidence.

data.title: Macquarie Small Cap Growth Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

It was a rough start to 2025 for the market and small-cap growth stocks. The Russell 2000 Growth Index declined 11.1% for 1Q25 and was the weakest-performing index among small-, mid-, and large-cap stocks. Macroeconomic fears dominated the market sentiment, and because small-growth stocks are the category with the highest volatility, they are affected the most in these downturns. The underperformance of small-cap stocks relative to large-cap stocks is reaching historic levels, and this may result in a sustained period of premium returns in the near future. The uncertainty created by the on-again, off-again tariff situation by the federal government and its impact on the economy and inflation will be a rough patch to navigate over the near term. Once the dust settles and earnings predictability improves, the attractive valuation of small-cap stocks should lead to a better return environment.

For 1Q25, information technology (IT), consumer discretionary, and communication services were the weakest-performing sectors. On the flipside, consumer staples, utilities, and healthcare were the strongest-performing sectors. Financials and real estate also outperformed in the benchmark. From a factor perspective, higher return on equity companies and lower valuation stocks outperformed.

Within the Fund

For 1Q25, Macquarie Small Cap Growth Fund (IYSIX) Institutional Class shares declined but outperformed the Fund’s benchmark, the Russell 2000 Growth Index.

The Fund was in the top half of its peer group for the quarter. Healthcare, IT, financials, communication services, and consumer discretionary were all outperforming sectors in the Fund. Industrials lagged and consumer staples was a modest underperformer. The healthcare sector generated positive absolute performance, thereby producing the strongest relative sector contribution. Product-specific strength continued in areas still rebounding from previous COVID-19 closures and headwinds. For instance, ANI Pharmaceuticals Inc. (ANIP) increased marketing and physician awareness to produce a robust upside in sales. However, the sector is clearly uneasy about the Trump administration’s newly appointed department heads and policy risk along with sticky inflation and interest rate pressures, especially on non-earning and non-cash-flow stocks. The outperforming pharmaceuticals segment was the largest contributor in 1Q due to a significant overweight relative to the benchmark and better stock selection, including Axsome Therapeutics Inc. (AXSM) on strong sales growth and positive clinical data. Within healthcare providers, GeneDx Holdings Corp. (WGS) contributed strongly to performance following a robust uptake of its new genome and exome pediatric tests.

The Fund’s IT sector outperformed the benchmark due to stock selection, which offset the drag from an overweight position in what was an underperforming sector. OSI Systems Inc. (OSIS), CyberArk Software Ltd. (CYBR), and Rubrik Inc. (RBRK) were among the winners, offsetting declines from Agilysys Inc. (AGYS) and Lumentum Holdings Inc. (LITE) OSI continued to post strong sales and earnings performance from its primary cargo inspection and security screening systems. CyberArk and Rubrik are both cybersecurity software companies with leading positions in their segment of the market. Cyber spending has been very resilient in this market as the threats from security breaches remain elevated. On the downside, recent results from Agilysys were a modest disappointment from a timing perspective, which weighed on the stock. Lumentum, on the other hand, had delivered strong results, but the stock suffered from fears of a potential slowdown in data center spending, which has yet to materialize.

Palomar Holdings Inc. (PLMR) and Hamilton Lane Inc. (HLNE) were strong performers in the financials sector, which more than offset weakness at WisdomTree Inc. (WT) Palomar is primarily an earthquake insurer with steady results, and Hamilton Lane has been a fast-growing alternative investment manager. The weakness in exchange-traded fund provider WisdomTree simply reflected the market weakness for the period. Other notable winners in the quarter included Universal Technical Institute Inc. (UTI), Sportradar Group AG (SRAD), and Genius Sports Ltd. (GENI) in the consumer discretionary sector, and Imax (IMAX) in the communications services sector. UTI has a steady growing technical and healthcare training school network. Sportradar and Genius Sports are the two dominant providers of data to online casinos and other sports books globally. Imax’s global diversification is paying dividends for the company, as the quarter benefited from the blockbuster success of a local Chinese movie. Two apparel stocks lagged in the quarter and were sold from the Fund: Abercrombie & Fitch Co. (ANF) and Victoria’s Secret & Co. (VSCO)

In 1Q25, the Fund’s biggest underperformer was the industrials sector. Federal spending sensitivities, including outlooks for data center build; semiconductor and reshoring manufacturing; infrastructure bills and other federally sponsored stimulus;along with Department of Government Efficiency actions, dominated sector movements. The Fund was overweight many of these areas on the outlook that spending should enhance growth of many names. In general, we still hold this opinion, but the levels and timing of spend have certainly become less visible. Everus Construction Group Inc. (ECG), an electrical contractor with sizable data center awards, was a large detractor from performance after an unremarkable initial 2025 revenue projection. The aerospace and defense space was a bit brighter and contributed to performance during the quarter. Leonardo DRS Inc. (DRS) generated strong revenues and bookings from its electric power and propulsion systems.

Outlook

As mentioned above, macroeconomic issues are having the greatest influence on the market near term. The uncertainty created by the on-again, off-again tariff situation by the federal government is causing unnecessary volatility. Small-cap growth stocks have experienced a sharp correction from the November 2024 highs, and therefore we believe there are plenty of good-quality opportunities across sectors for the portfolio.
