data.title: FLXR: An Outperforming Multi-Sector Bond Fund
data.description:
Thesis

The TCW Group is a leading global asset management firm, dealing with a wide range of investment products across fixed income, alternative investments, equities, and emerging markets. We have not covered many TCW funds, but we came across a very exciting new entrant in the fixed income exchange traded funds space. We are talking here about the TCW Flexible Income ETF (NYSE:FLXR) which was listed in June 2024.

To note FLXR was originally the MetWest Flexible Income Fund, a mutual fund, before it was converted into the TCW Flexible Income ETF. The ETF adopted the performance history of the mutual fund, which had an inception date of November 30, 2018.

In today's article we are going to have a closer look at the fund and its composition and articulate why the name represents an attractive entrant in the multi-asset space.

What does FLXR do?

Let us have a look first at what the fund does:

The Fund seeks a high level of current income with a secondary objective of long-term capital appreciation. The TCW Flexible Income ETF (FLXR) has the flexibility to invest across sectors, allowing allocation shifts based on changing market conditions and relative value. The fund invests in a diversified mix of fixed income securities across various sectors, credit qualities, and maturities, both in the U.S. and globally.

To note that FLXR is an actively managed fund, and thus has the flexibility to enter and exit sectors as valuation bands change. The name thus represents the TCW take on multi-sector bond funds, a cohort which we have covered extensively:

  • JPIE: Is Still A Buy
  • PYLD: PIMCO's Active ETF Is Still Growing And Offering Attractive Returns
  • CGMS: An Attractive Opportunity In The Fixed Income Space

The ETF conversion has worked

We have seen many mutual fund to ETF conversions as of late, due to the liquidity offered by the ETF product which increases institutional interest in the respective fund. It is the case here as well, with a positive result:

The fund's AUM has experienced a 4x growth in the past year, with the assets under management now just shy of $1.4 billion. Kindly remember that more and more institutional players use ETFs as building blocks, and when it comes to risk calculations and liquidity (i.e. ability to exit positions), the differences between continuously priced ETFs versus end of the day NAV for mutual funds are significant.

Also please keep in mind that while the structure of the fund has changed from a legal and intra-day liquidity standpoint, the underlying risks and performances have not. The management team has remained, thus pre-conversion risk analytics and total return figures are very much valid.

Current fund composition

Let us now look at how the holdings for the fund are currently positioned:

The name is overweight MBS bonds via its 'Agency MBS' and 'Non-Agency MBS' sleeves. The two sectors combined have an approximate 35% allocation. ABS is the next largest sleeve at 19%, followed by High Yield Credit at 15%. Given the high allocation to rate sensitive sectors (including Government) we believe rates is the main risk factor here.

We very much like the comparison in allocations with the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, which as we can see above is overweight treasuries, and has no HY allocations.

From an analytics perspective here are the figures for the ETF:

The fund is very granular with over 1400 securities, and sports a low duration of 3.12 years. We love this duration bucket because it insulates funds from the issues associated with the long end of the treasuries curve. Kindly note how active the fund is with a portfolio turnover of over 300%.

When compared to the Aggregate Bond Index, the ETF is shorter in duration and contains a lower number of securities (while still granular nonetheless).

Performance metrics

Let us now look at how the fund has done historically:

Since the fund has income as its primary objective we are using a total return graph above. We are comparing the fund with a cohort of peers, which includes the JPMorgan Income ETF (JPIE), the PIMCO Multisector Bond Active ETF (PYLD) and the Capital Group US Multi-Sector ETF (CGMS). We have covered all these funds in the past.

We can see that FLXR is a clear outperformer, with the highest return over the past three years. The cohort is not fungible since each fund takes a slightly different duration and allocation profile, but they all target the multi-sector space. Ultimately for these names performance is the differentiator, since it represents the result of the choices made by the respective portfolio managers. And FLXR has indeed outperformed.

Let us look at a drawdown profile as well to see if FLXR is taking more risk than its peers:

We can see that in the past year CGMS is actually the riskiest fund, with the largest drawdown in April 2025. Below some further analytics for the name:

  • 30-day SEC yield: 5.5%
  • 1Y Total Return: 9.5%
  • Annualized vol: 3.2%

Macro set-up and FLXR

As we have said in many articles before, we believe credit spreads are too narrow - i.e. investors do not get compensated enough for the credit risk they are taking. We like how FLXR has a small allocation to corporate IG and HY credit, while overweight MBS products. This set-up helps performance if the Fed cuts aggressively, while keeping drawdowns controlled if there is another credit event. We clearly saw that dynamic in April 2025 when the fund had a small -1.7% drawdown, while CGMS was down -4%.

Active ETFs such as FLXR will be able to allocate capital among the various fixed income sleeves as they see fit from a valuation standpoint, thus investors benefit fully from the portfolio manager's experience.

Conclusion

FLXR is a multi-sector fixed income ETF from TCW. The name has done very well in the past year, posting a total return in excess of 9% all while quadrupling assets under management. The ETF represents a mutual fund to ETF conversion and brings to the table TCW's experience in the fixed income arena. With appealing risk/reward metrics and a solid management team, FLXR is a 'Buy' in today's macro environment.

data.title: McDonald's: Fairly Valued Today, With Solid Upside On Execution
data.description:
Introduction

Known for its big yellow "M" sign and operating over 43,000 restaurants in over 100 countries, serving 70 million customers every single day, McDonald's Corp. (NYSE:MCD) is not the type of company that needs an extensive introduction.

In recent months, however, just like Starbucks Corp. (SBUX), which I've just covered, they received little love from investors - deservedly so, as growth rates have plummeted and even turned slightly negative. The stock, in turn, is now down approximately 10% from its ATH, barely noticeable on the long-term chart.

Historically, though, McDonald's has been a great compounder that combines a strong brand, large international exposure, and dedicated capital returns for shareholders. As I do not expect these aspects to meaningfully change within the next few years, I am arguing that this dip is a buying opportunity more than anything else. McDonald's has some catalysts that seem able to revive growth.

Why McDonalds?

The global fast-food market is expected to reach $1T in 2034, growing at 3.7% per year. If McDonald's simply maintains their market share, which I believe they can due to possessing incredibly strong brand and network effects, they could grow at set rate.

Q1 FY2025 financials were rough. Here are some developments:

  • Total consolidated revenue shrunk 3% YoY with global comparable sales and systemwide sales declining by 1% each amid softness in store traffic
  • Operating income dropped 3% and EPS 2% (if netting off one-time restructuring charges of $66M and $35M, these metrics come in at -2% and -1%)
  • All of these metrics look less significant on a CC basis, most of them flat and some even reflecting growth

Long-term guidance

  • continuous operating margin expansion, countering voices of cost pressures
  • increases of CapEx from $300M to $500M each year for the next 2 years, mainly used for new store growth
  • Regarding new store growth, they target 50,000 global units by 2027

2022 inflation was met with heavy price increases, which do not seem feasible anymore for the fast food chain, as consumer spending has slowed and the company had to release a lot of budget options, like the meal deal. Instead, according to their 10-K, they are primarily focused on three growth pillars: Maximizing their Marketing, Committing to their Core and Doubling Down on the 4Ds.

In order to maximize their Marketing, they will focus on their value proposition, providing affordable and delicious menu options and limited time deals, scale their feel-good Marketing around the globe to connect with more consumers and embrace bold creatives to communicate the story of their brand, food, and purpose to stand out.

Arguably more important is that they are committing to core growth, meaning they focus on their customer favorites in driving value, by making "hotter, juicier and tastier burgers" and menu innovation where they are focused on gaining market share in Chicken through scaling their emerging favorites McCrispy and McSpicy .

The 4 D's are the following:

  • Digital: Making more use of their app with over 175M 90-day active users. I see the most potential in this category, as more engaged users should order more frequently and at higher average ticket sizes
  • Scaling Delivery with strategic partnerships
  • Improve and penetrate Drive Thru: In their IOM segment, 80% of restaurants offer a drive through, and in their IDL segment it is less than 50%, compared to 95% in the US, indicating potential for expansion
  • Development: Expansion is set to reach the fastet period of restaurant unit growth in company history.

Further, the December 2023 pilot project CosMc's has been put to an end, closing all five experimental stores. What many might interpret as a failure, I believe that McDonald's did gain a lot of data and lessons from this experiment. They are going to incorporate some of the popular drinks from CosMc's into their existing stores in order to increase afternoon market penetration. I view this as an additional potential growth catalyst. They will also be competing more directly with Starbucks in that regard and might be able to take some market share from them if they're able to match the quality of drinks at cheaper prices.

Regarding leverage, McDonald's is on a 4-year downtrend with their Debt/EBITDA levels, now at 2.7X, which is manageable. They also carry solid credit ratings, making me rather unconcerned about current debt levels.

Leveraging their decent financial position, they return FCF to shareholders rather than paying back all debt as fast as possible, which I do like. Most of cash goes towards dividends and the "rest of it" goes towards share repurchases. This is a healthy mix. At a 2.5% yield and a 61% payout ratio, the dividend seems safe and even poised for solid single digit growth for the next few years.

Why now?

Historically, current valuation levels seem fair as McDonald's are neither extremely over- nor undervalued. When comparing historical growth rates to historical valuations, however, we are in the pricey territory at the moment, as growth has been taking a toll recently and relative valuations staying within the normal range. This could be a sign of the market partially pricing in current circumstances to be temporary.

On a forward P/E ratio, the company looks fairly valued, trailing at 23.8X. McDonald's also seems fairly valued in a peer comparison with Restaurant Brands (QSR), Wendy's (WEN), Domino's (DPZ), Chipotle (CMG), and Starbucks (SBUX), trading around the mid-range of set group.

Since McDonald's is certain not to deliver CAGRs of 20%+, one can turn to technicals to improve their returns from MCD by buying when the stock is oversold or (far) below its 400 day MA. Buying at lower points will increase price returns in the long run, as well as offering a higher dividend yield, boosting total return. On the monthly chart, MCD is almost below this moving average and at a monthly RSI of 51, indicating a good timing to commit to a first entry.

In order to predict potential future returns, I will do a scenario analysis with three cases (bear, base, bull) based on different assumptions.

Bear Case

  • McDonald's are continuously facing a hard economic environment and lose some market share.
  • In turn, they only grow 2%, slower than the market's growth rate
  • Margins remain the same (due to higher costs) despite management guiding for increases in operating margins. Therefore, net income also grows 2% p.a.
  • Share repurchases increase EPS growth to 3.5%.
  • They trade at a lower P/E of 20X
  • 2030 share price would reach $280, returning negative 0.6% per year

Base Case

  • McDonald's grows revenues at the market rate of 3.7%
  • Margins will be expanded, but only slightly, leading to a net income margin of 35%.
  • The above combined with share repurchases (reducing the share count by 1.1% per year = 670M shares) makes EPS reach $16.80
  • The market trades McDonald's at a similar P/E as today, 25X
  • 2030 share price grows to $421, implying a CAGR of 6.3%

Bull Case

  • McDonald's manages to grow at 6%, which is above the market rate due to store growth, international expansion, higher app usage (leading to more orders and higher AOV) and higher global drive thru penetration
  • They manage to expand their operating margins to 49% (the upper end of their guidance), leading to a simultaneous increase in net income margin to 37%.
  • 2030 EPS jumps to $20.3 when accounting for buybacks as above
  • The market rewards McDonald's with a P/E of 27X, as it surpassed its expectations and share price hits $568.5
  • The stock would return 11.1% from today.

Keeping in mind that a dividend of 2.5% (and growing) is added to these performances, MCD are starting to look compelling for a first entry.

Risks

Risks include that slow growth is not just a temporary issue but persists, which would make McDonald's a value trap with high opportunity costs. The next run up in the S&P500 could very well be an AI-drive rally, which other companies can benefit much more directly from, potentially making new investors of McDonald's miss out on a lot of those gains.

Conclusion

McDonald's looks like it offers decent value at these levels which makes me rate them a cautious Buy. Starting a first position now and adding on further weakness seems like the right course of action. With a beta of 0.56, McDonalds is well positioned as a portfolio addition that lowers overall volatility, especially during difficult times. In the meantime, investors are rewarded with a growing 2.5% dividend yield.

data.title: NUMG: Relative Results Should Improve For This ESG Mid-Cap Growth ETF
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The Nuveen ESG Mid-Cap Growth ETF (BATS:NUMG) provides consistent exposure to mid-cap growth stocks that pass a specific and transparent environmental, social, and governance screening process. With an affordable 0.31% expense ratio, NUMG seems like a good choice, but unfortunately, it's struggled against its peers over the last few years, so the purpose of this article is to determine why and whether that will continue. In my view, the problem is isolated to a few select stocks that will likely graduate to large-cap indices shortly, so despite its disappointing result, I see competitive returns moving forward, and I look forward to explaining why in further detail below.

NUMG Overview

Strategy Discussion

NUMG tracks the Nuveen ESG USA Mid-Cap Growth Index, which MSCI describes as follows:

The Nuveen ESG USA Mid-Cap Growth Index is based on the MSCI USA Mid-Cap Growth Index, its parent Index, which captures mid cap securities exhibiting overall growth style characteristics in the US. The Nuveen ESG USA Mid-Cap Growth Index is designed to represent the performance of a Nuveen strategy that aims to increase the Index's exposure to positive environmental, social and governance factors as well as exhibit lower carbon exposure than the Parent Index. The Index also aims to minimize the tracking error relative to the parent Index through an optimization process.

The Index excludes several stocks that don't meet Nuveen's ESG criteria. These include companies with "enhanced controversy scores," as determined by MSCI, as well as those involved in the alcohol, tobacco, gambling, nuclear power, and weapons and firearms industries. The Index also excludes companies with missing or "CCC" MSCI ESG Ratings and certain ones based on their carbon emission intensities.

Key to the optimization process is a set of constraints, which are summarized in the table below. As shown, each constituent's weight is limited to +/- 1.25% compared to the Parent Index, and the total weight per sector is within 4%, except 8% for Energy. There are also constraints for turnover, specific risk, and common factor risk, all with the goal of reducing tracking error to 3%.

Please note that Nuveen's Index does not apply any additional fundamental screens beyond what's necessary to qualify for the MSCI USA Mid Cap Growth Index. However, the screening process for that Index is comprehensive and assesses a company's short and long-term forward EPS growth rate, current internal growth rate, and long-term historical sales and EPS growth trends.

Performance Snapshot

To begin, consider the following table showing how the ESG Index performed against its Parent Index between May 31, 2012, and May 30, 2025. These are Index returns, meaning they do not account for fund expense ratios, but they highlight how the ESG Index trailed over the last three, five, and ten years. The second table also suggests investors can expect higher turnover, lower risk-adjusted returns, and larger drawdowns - neither of which are positive.

Still, the next chart and table show that the ESG Index wasn't always a poor performer. For example, it outperformed for four consecutive years between 2013 and 2016, held up reasonably well in the 2018 market decline, and outperformed by over 4% in 2020. It's really only been the last 3-4 years where it's struggled, so I think it's important that we take some time to understand why. After all, this is a mid-cap fund, so stocks contributing to the Parent Index's strong returns might be graduating to a large-cap Index soon if they haven't already. If that's the case, these historical returns might not mean much.

Peer Group Analysis

In this article, I will analyze NUMG against four peers, as follows:

  1. American Century Mid Cap Growth Impact ETF (MID)
  2. Invesco ESG NASDAQ Next Gen 100 ETF (QQJG)
  3. iShares Russell Mid-Cap Growth ETF (IWP)
  4. Vanguard Mid-Cap Growth Index ETF (VOT)

MID and QQJG represent two newer ESG options in the mid-cap growth space, while IWP and VOT are the two largest by assets under management and have cheaper expense ratios of 0.23% and 0.07%, respectively. As you might expect, all four have outperformed NUMG since QQJG's inception on October 27, 2021, with IWP and VOT delivering the best total returns of 19.09% and 14.93%, respectively. This finding indicates that ESG stocks in the mid-cap growth space have struggled, not just the ones in NUMG.

NUMG Analysis

Key Exclusions and Size Distribution

These recent performance differences are alarming, but they're actually easily explained by just a handful of stocks, several of which won't be in IWP and VOT shortly. I've identified the following six stocks poised to graduate to large-cap growth ETFs shortly, given how their market caps are at least $80 billion. Furthermore, they've gained 578% on average over the last five years, yet neither are held by either NUMG, MID, or QQJG:

  1. Palantir Technologies (PLTR): $340B Market Cap, 1259% 5Y Total Return
  2. AppLovin (APP): $118B Market Cap, 771% 5Y Total Return
  3. DoorDash (DASH): $102B Market Cap, 240% 5Y Total Return
  4. Constellation Energy (CEG): $101B Market Cap, 453% 5Y Total Return
  5. Coinbase Global (COIN): $96B Market Cap, 532% 5Y Total Return
  6. TransDigm Group (TDG): $83B Market Cap, 216% 5Y Total Return

In particular, Palantir Technologies now comprises 9.03% of IWP, so after Russell Indexes completes its annual reconstitution process, it will be removed. Russell will also add a second reconstitution starting in November, so these types of "problems" won't happen as frequently and should lead to better tracking of the mid-cap universe. That should also help level the playing field because, as of today, 16-21% of companies by weight in IWP and VOT are large-cap stocks, defined as ones with market caps between $71B and $350B. NUMG is the only "true" mid-cap fund, with all holdings having market caps between $12B and $71B.

For those curious, I calculated these breakpoints based on the current market cap distribution of the total U.S. stock market (Cumulative Top 40% = Giant, Next 30% = Large, Next 20% = Mid, Next 7% = Small, Next 3% = Micro).

Sector Allocations vs. Peer Group

The following table highlights the sector allocation differences between NUMG, MID, QQJG, IWP, and VOT. Again, these are subject to change as each ETF's underlying Index reconstitutes, but they show NUMG is reasonably well diversified at the sector level. Its largest exposure is to Technology at 27.23%, followed by Industrials and Health Care at 21.17% and 13.76%, respectively.

NUMG Fundamental Analysis

The next table highlights selected fundamental metrics for NUMG's top 25 holdings, which comprise 68.28% of the portfolio. These metrics are centered around growth, value, and quality, and in my opinion, indicate that NUMG is competitive with IWP and VOT across the board.

Here are two takeaways to consider:

NUMG: Excellent Growth Factor Exposure, Reasonable Valuation

The table highlights how NUMG's components grew sales and earnings per share by 14.27% and 18.02% per year over the last three years, which is about as good as IWP and VOT. MID is also a good choice for mid-cap growth, though it's a semi-transparent ETF, so I can't be certain that I've captured its holdings accurately. Lastly, QQJG's 10.26% and 7.87% historical sales and earnings growth rates are substantially lower, so despite it being a "next-gen" ETF, these growth rates suggest they're a long way away from qualifying for the larger Nasdaq-100 Index.

NUMG is also competitive on one-year estimated sales and earnings per share growth, and its 27.24x forward P/E ratio makes it reasonably attractive from a growth-at-a-reasonable-price perspective. To illustrate, here is a modified PEG ratio calculations from dividing each ETF's estimated earnings growth rate into their respective forward P/E ratios:

  • NUMG: 27.24x / 18.91% = 1.44x
  • MID: 30.67x / 19.19% = 1.60x
  • QQJG: 19.14x / 17.16% = 1.12x
  • IWP: 28.66x / 21.93% = 1.31x
  • VOT: 30.43x / 17.91% = 1.70x

And when using each ETF's trailing P/E ratio and three-year historical earnings per share growth rate:

  • NUMG: 30.06x / 18.02% = 1.67x
  • MID: 30.99x / 20.75% = 1.49x
  • QQJG: 22.75x / 7.87% = 2.89x
  • IWP: 32.26x / 17.92% = 1.80x
  • VOT: 33.66x / 19.12% = 1.76x

As these calculations show, NUMG ranks #3/5 on forward-looking metrics and #2/5 on historical-looking metrics, so its growth and valuation combination is pretty solid.

NUMG: Quality Metrics Also Solid

There are signs that NUMG is a high-quality ETF. Its 23.28% and 11.84% ROE and ROTC figures indicate strong and efficient operational performance, and again, you'll find these are in line with the more popular IWP and VOT. Similarly, its 8.04/10 profit score, which I derived from Seeking Alpha Factor Grades, is also competitive and is supported by additional quality statistics listed in the table below.

A few quick notes:

  • NUMG's 0.89 average asset turnover ratio is excellent, demonstrating an efficient use of assets to generate sales.
  • NUMG's debt metrics are favorable across the board, including current ratio, debt-equity, debt-free cash flow, and interest coverage.
  • NUMG's 8.44% net margins are low, but its 16.36% free cash flow margins are above the peer group average and in line with IWP and VOT.

Investment Recommendation

NUMG is competitive with IWP and VOT on key statistics covering the growth, value, and quality factors, and its relatively low 27.24x forward P/E could give it a slight edge moving forward. While you might be tempted to conclude from past performance that NUMG's ESG focus has harmed investors, the more accurate reason is the outstanding success of a few select stocks, namely Palantir Technologies, AppLovin, and Coinbase Global, which will soon move to large-cap indices, if they haven't already. Furthermore, Russell Indexes will add a second reconstitution starting this November, which should cut down on the performance difference between NUMG and IWP.

To be sure, NUMG isn't a "home run" by any stretch, and I have little interest in ESG funds in general, as they often come with higher fees. Still, NUMG's fundamentals are solid, and I'm not too concerned with its past performance, so I've decided to assign it a "hold" rating. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Canadian National Railway: Pullback Is An Excellent Opportunity
data.description:
As a value investor, I get more excited when quality stocks go down than when they go up. That’s because lower prices mean that my capital is working harder for me through the ability to acquire more shares at decent prices. Such I find the case to be with Canadian National Railway (NYSE:CNI), which I last covered in January 2023, highlighting its strong fundamentals and fair valuation.

My last article’s title: "Canadian National Railway: Wait For A Pullback", demonstrates that it pays to be patient, as shares have pulled back by 14% since my last piece. At the current price of $103, CNI trades at a reasonable forward PE of 18 (compared to its growth prospects) and sits solidly in the bottom half of its 52-week trading range, as shown below.

Looking at a longer time frame, CNI has bested the S&P 500 (SPY) for much of the past 10 years until recently starting in 2024.

In this article, I revisit CNI including recent business results, and discuss why the pullback in price makes it attractive for value investors seeking to layer into this long-term growth stock, so let’s get started!

Why CNI?

Canadian National Railway is one of North America’s largest Class I railroads. It operates a 19,500-mile network that spans across Canada, and the U.S. Midwest and Gulf Coast. CNI’s diversified freight mix includes intermodal, grain, coal, petroleum & chemicals, metals, and automotive.

CNI is well known for its operational efficiency and strong pricing power due to its strategic network access to key ports like Prince Rupert, Vancouver, and the Gulf Coast. As shown below, CNI scores an A+ grade for Profitability. This includes a strong net income margin and return on equity of 26% and 22%, respectively.

Meanwhile, CNI generated respectable Q1 2025 results, with revenue growing by 4% YoY. This was driven by solid pricing and strong performances in grain, fertilizers, coal, and automotive segments, partially offset by softer volumes in iron ore and intermodal, as shown below.

Notably, CNI’s strong pricing resulted in operating ratio (as defined by operating expenses as a percentage of revenue) declining by 20 bps YoY to 63.4%, and this contributed to operating income growth of 4% YoY to C$1.61 billion.

Notably, CNI was able to overcome adverse weather events earlier in the year, with a strong rebound in March, moving nearly 1.4 billion daily gross ton miles. This marks one of the strongest performances for March in company history. Moreover, train car velocity improved to 200 miles per day, and dwell times dropped to just 7.5 hours per day. This reflects operational efficiencies and disciplined resource utilization management across the fleet.

Management is guiding for a robust full year EPS growth of 12.5% at the midpoint of range (10-15%). This is supported by the recent conclusion of arbitration for Canadian conductors and engineers, which secured a three-year deal with manageable wage increases. Labor productivity improved by 2% YoY as CNI internalized core engineering work. Moreover, management’s outlook includes growth initiatives including intermodal expansions, met coal exports, new crush plants, and sand terminals. Specifically, CNI expects to see intermodal growth being weighted toward the second half of this year.

CNI carries a strong balance sheet with an A- credit rating from S&P. This is supported by $161 million in cash on the balance sheet and a safe net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 2.55x, sitting in-line with management’s 2.5x target. CNI currently yields 2.5% and the dividend is well-covered by a 45% payout ratio. It also comes with a 5-year CAGR of 15% and 28 consecutive years of growth.

Lastly, I find CNI to be appealing at the current price of $103.35 with a forward PE of 18.4, sitting slightly below its normal PE of 18.8, as shown below.

I’m upgrading CNI from a ‘Hold’ to ‘Buy’, given the pullback in price over the past 12 months. With the shares trading at CNI’s historical valuation, it’s well-positioned to deliver potential market-beating total returns. This is considering CNI’s long-track record of outsized total returns until recent times, and forward analyst EPS growth estimates in the 10% to 12% range annually over the next two years. I believe this is reasonable considering the recent resolution of arbitration with employees, high productivity on its train cars, and intermodal growth. EPS growth, combined with the 2.5% yield, could result in double-digit total returns annually for shareholders.

Risks to CNI include potential for tariffs and trade disruptions as a result of negotiations between the U.S. and Canada. Plus, a shift in supply chains away from Asia could negatively impact CNI’s intermodal volumes, particularly at Prince Rupert. Plus, weather-related risks include harsh winters in Canada, which could hurt efficiency.

Investor Takeaway

Canadian National Railway presents an attractive opportunity for value investors following a pullback in price over the past 12 months. At the current price of $103.35 and a forward P/E of 18.4, CNI trades slightly below its historical valuation and has solid fundamentals.

Plus, CNI demonstrates strong profitability, efficient operations, and a resilient freight mix. Looking ahead, CNI is well-positioned to deliver strong total returns. This is backed by a healthy balance sheet, a safe 2.5% dividend yield, and clear long-term growth drivers like intermodal expansion. Considering all the above, I’m upgrading CNI to a ‘Buy’ rating for its long-term value from here.

data.title: Anterix: Uniquely Positioned To Ride The Wave Of Energy Infrastructure Growth
data.description:
Investment thesis

Anterix (NASDAQ:ATEX) has a market cap of close to half a billion, but remains largely unknown among investors and has low analyst coverage. The company has a unique license asset in the 900 MHz spectrum. Its target customers are from the utility sector, which is seeing a massive increase in infrastructure related spending.

Meanwhile, because the revenue from the leases signed with its customers are recognized ratably over the lease period, even as the entire cash amount is received in advance, typical earnings metrics and analyst estimates are less relevant for investors to track. Instead, investors ought to focus on the magnitude of cash flows that the business is set to earn in the coming years, along with the associated operating expenses.

I have identified meaningful risks such as potential competition from 800 MHz as well as the potential for delays, which makes this an investment only for investors comfortable with the higher risk profile. While the current valuation implies a steep discount compared to the underlying asset value, I am recommending the stock as a speculative Buy.

Anterix overview and financial highlights

Anterix is the largest holder of 900 MHz licensed spectrum in the US, having received authorization from the FCC for the 3x3 MHz broadband block (6 MHz of broadband spectrum) in May 2020. As the owner and operator of this license, the company aims to facilitate the building of private LTE broadband networks that utilities could use. The trends of energy transition to clean energy sources, decentralization of the grid, electrification as well as the growing energy demand from data centers are all contributing towards the need for utilities to have their own broadband network.

In order to increase the services offered on its platform and grow the associated revenue opportunity, Anterix has engaged with over 125 partners who can offer their products and services on the platform. This includes 20 technology companies like Ericsson (ERIC), General Electric (GE) and Nokia (NOK).

Turning to its business model, the company earns its revenue through the sale or lease of segments of its license. Payment for these leases, which are typically for around 20 years, is received in cash upfront during the first three years after the contract is signed. Despite receiving the cash in advance, revenue is recognized ratably and therefore quarterly metrics and analyst estimates carry no meaning when analyzing the business. Instead, investors need to estimate the number of contracts that will be signed during a period and the associated cash inflow from them, and subsequently offset this against the expected operating expenses, including platform development and clearing costs associated with the spectrum.

So far, the company has signed contracts with seven utility customers, including Xcel Energy (XEL), Ameren (AEE) and the recently signed $102.5 million broadband spectrum sale to Oncor Electric throughout its service territory. These agreements so far have been for $390 million in contracted proceeds, with nearly $150 million still outstanding, most of which will be received during FY26. Meanwhile, its balance sheet currently consists of $47 million in cash and no debt.

Outlook and risks

Anterix is enjoying tailwinds from the utility sector where according to a recent report by S&P Global (SPGI), US-based utilities are forecasted to spend over $212 billion this year on infrastructure enhancements, a 22% increase compared to 2024. This growth is expected to remain strong until 2027.

This important slide from the latest investor presentation, which management updates on a quarterly basis, can be used to track the size of the company's pipeline along with the contract value and demonstrated intent from potential customers. The total pipeline exceeds $3 billion, with approximately $1 billion considered to be near term opportunities from deals with 18 potential customers. Management's guidance for this year, however, was slightly tempered with no specific figures and commentary on the Q4 earnings calls plainly suggesting a significant increase from the $116 million in contracted proceeds that was realized in FY25. Given the massive pipeline that the company currently has, I believe this guidance has disappointed investors, who would have expected a higher proportion of the pipeline to be converted to realized contracts already in the coming fiscal year.

A key risk which emerged recently was the agreement reached by Grain with T-Mobile US (TMUS) to purchase the 800 MHz spectrum. In their letter to the FCC, Grain has already mentioned their intention to work with utilities and would therefore directly compete with Anterix. While Anterix has the upper hand given its head start and already proven deployments with seven utilities, it will potentially have to offer its future customers competitive pricing if Grain offers a viable alternative.

In an effort to strengthen its offering, Anterix recently announced its intent to invest up to $250 million through the AnterixAccelerator program to bring on additional features to the platform, while also preparing for an expansion to the 5x5 MHz (10 MHz depth) spectrum. For investors who were previously attracted to the investment case due to the need for low capital investments, this is a concern, as cash now needs to be deployed into enhancing the product, which instead could have been returned to shareholders. Discussing the competitive threat from 800 MHz on the Q4 earnings call, Anterix's CEO stated:

We are confident that our economics, along with our market leadership of actual deployments and our long-term commitment to the industry will continue to be the utilities preferred choice.

Another risk for investors is related to extensions in the timeline for crucial events. The expansion to 5x5 MHz is a vital catalyst which would increase the value of its assets and its appeal to potential customers. There was a positive announcement regarding it from the FCC in January, yet the timeline for approval remains unclear.

Additionally, while the current pipeline is massive, capex decisions made by utility owners tend to get drawn out, and there is a possibility that the pipeline opportunities take longer to be converted to realized contracts.

My approach to valuing ATEX stock

A straightforward method to arrive at an estimate for the value of the licenses held by Anterix is to use MHz-POP Pricing. This involves multiplying the MHz-POP price, the broadband spectrum (6MHz in this case) and the population covered. Assuming $1.83 per MHz-POP and a population of 280 million (~10% of 312 million people, which is the company's total coverage has already been sold or leased), I get a valuation of $3.07 billion. This is more than 6 times larger than its current market cap. I believe my estimate of $1.83 per MHz-POP is reasonable, as it is based on last year's deal with Oncor, where Anterix received $102.5 million for 9.3 million POPs in Texas. Moreover, this estimate lies between the $0.93 per MHz-POP price for the 600 MHz licenses and the $2.72 per MHz-POP price for the Advanced Wireless Services ("AWS-3"). Pricing should continue to trend higher as the platform size increases and more partners are included to increase the technical offerings on the platform.

Besides this, I will approach the valuation of ATEX through a discounted cash flow approach, where I will estimate the cumulative FCF from the business in the next five years, from just the 3x3 MHz broadband segment alone. Based on the current pipeline which I discussed earlier, let's assume $1 billion in cash is received during the next five years, from either sales or leases. When it comes to expenses, I model $45 million in cash operating expenses for FY26, including $20 million for the final portion of clearing costs. This is followed by $25 million in annual operating expenses for the subsequent four years. Since the company has more than $300 million in Federal NOLs, I assume no tax expenses. I assume $12 million in cash spent annually on share repurchases just to offset the dilution from stock-based compensation.

Thus, I end up with $1 billion in total expected cash payments to the business over the next five years, with total operating expenses cumulatively adding up to $205 million. This results in $795 million of cumulative FCF, to which I apply a discount rate of 15% to arrive at a fair value to pay today. While my discount rate may seem high, I believe my five-year time horizon and high discount rate is justified given the elevated risk profile. Nevertheless, I arrive at a fair value of $397 million, which when combined with the $150 million in outstanding contracted proceeds and $47 million in cash on the balance sheet, results in $594 million of total value. This translates to a share price of $31.8, which represents an upside of 23%.

Next, I will attempt to factor in the upside from the potential expansion to 5x5 MHz, which the company is already investing for. I believe FCC approval for 5x5 MHz would significantly improve the value of its licenses. Simply replacing the 6 MHz broadband segment with 10 MHz should increase the license value by 66%, at least on paper. An aspect that needs to be considered here however is the clearing costs to move incumbents out. Therefore, when adopting my FCF based approach as before, I will include an additional $350 million in cumulative operating expenses over the next five years, including the $250 million committed towards the AnterixAccelerator program. This would increase the total expenses over the period to $555 million. Meanwhile, I expect the 5x5 MHz expansion to translate more contracts, which would also be larger in size. Cumulative cash payments to Anterix could end up at close to $1.5 billion over the next five years, which would result in a cumulative FCF of $945 million. When discounted at a rate of 15%, I get a present value of $472 million, or a total value of $669 million after adding the outstanding contracted proceeds and cash which I mentioned earlier. This translates to a share price of $35.8, for an upside of 39%.

While this upside may not seem massive, I will point out that my estimates are fairly conservative and assume that the company will only tap a fraction of the total market opportunity available to it. I have also implicitly assigned no value for the cash flows from the business beyond the next five years, though in reality the company should be able to continue to increase the value of its platform and bring in cash from new sales and leases as well as when prior leases are renewed.

What makes the risk reward appealing at this time is that while the stock is trading at a discount, the company has engaged Morgan Stanley (MS) to conduct a formal strategic review following the inbound interest that it had received. While this may not result in the buyout of the company, I think there remains a reasonable probability for this to occur, given that its largest shareholder, Owl Creek, owns 29% of the company and has representation on the board. Owl Creek increased their stake in Anterix as recent as Q4 last year.

Conclusion

ATEX stock trades at an attractive valuation and at a large discount to the value of its licenses, with the 5x5 MHz expansion as a potential positive catalyst ahead. Execution by management has been solid so far and the outlook remains promising, given the tailwinds related to utility infrastructure spending growth. On the other hand, the competition from 800 MHz is a key risk, which could potentially pressure pricing and force the company to make additional investments in order to further differentiate its offering. Given the higher risk profile, I consider the stock a speculative buy.
